title: Project Istanbul
text:
2.1 Data Source
Deprem Senaryosu Analiz Sonuçları
İstanbul Çevresinde Gerçekleşen Depremler 2019-2020
2019-2024 Yılları Belediye Nüfusları
2017 Mahalle Bazlı Bina Sayıları
VDYM İlçe Bazında Hanelerdeki Kentsel Dönüşüm Fikri Bireysel Öngörüleri is a dataset about the opinions of households about the idea of urban transformation on a district basis.
All this data taken from İstanbul Büyükşehir Belediyesi Açık Veri Portalı
District boundary geometries used for mapping were obtained from GADM version 4.1.


Show Code
# Import the five raw Excel datasets.
depremsenaryo <- read_excel("depremsenaryo.xlsx")
mahbinasayi <- read_excel("mahbinasayi.xlsx")
istanbuldepremverisi <- read_excel(
  "istanbuldepremverisi.xlsx",
  sheet = "istanbuldepremverisi"
)
belediyenufus <- read_excel("belediyenufus.xlsx")
kentseldonusum <- read_excel("kentseldonusum.xlsx")

# Convert character fields to UTF-8 and remove unnecessary whitespace.
clean_text <- function(x) {
  x |>
    enc2utf8() |>
    str_squish() |>
    na_if("")
}

# Create a common district key for later merging.
clean_district <- function(x) {
  x |>
    clean_text() |>
    str_remove(regex("\\s+Belediyesi$", ignore_case = TRUE)) |>
    str_to_upper(locale = "tr") |>
    recode("EYÜP" = "EYÜPSULTAN")
}

depremsenaryo <- depremsenaryo |>
  mutate(
    across(where(is.character), clean_text),
    ilce_adi = clean_district(ilce_adi)
  ) |>
  distinct()

mahbinasayi <- mahbinasayi |>
  mutate(
    across(where(is.character), clean_text),
    ilce_adi = clean_district(ilce_adi)
  ) |>
  distinct()

istanbuldepremverisi <- istanbuldepremverisi |>
  mutate(
    across(where(is.character), clean_text),
    time = as.POSIXct(
      Time,
      format = "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%OSZ",
      tz = "UTC"
    ),
    Magnitude = as.numeric(Magnitude),
    `Depth/km` = as.numeric(`Depth/km`),
    Latitude = as.numeric(Latitude),
    Longitude = as.numeric(Longitude)
  ) |>
  distinct()

belediyenufus <- belediyenufus |>
  mutate(
    across(where(is.character), clean_text),
    ilce_adi = clean_district(Belediyeler)
  ) |>
  distinct()

kentseldonusum <- kentseldonusum |>
  mutate(
    across(where(is.character), clean_text),
    ilce_adi = clean_district(Ilce)
  ) |>
  distinct()

# Confirm that the three district-level sources use the same 39 districts.
stopifnot(
  setequal(unique(depremsenaryo$ilce_adi), unique(mahbinasayi$ilce_adi)),
  setequal(unique(depremsenaryo$ilce_adi), unique(belediyenufus$ilce_adi)),
  setequal(unique(depremsenaryo$ilce_adi), unique(kentseldonusum$ilce_adi))
)

data_quality <- tibble(
  dataset = c(
    "Earthquake scenario",
    "Building stock",
    "Observed earthquakes",
    "District population",
    "Urban transformation"
  ),
  rows = c(
    nrow(depremsenaryo),
    nrow(mahbinasayi),
    nrow(istanbuldepremverisi),
    nrow(belediyenufus),
    nrow(kentseldonusum)
  ),
  columns = c(
    ncol(depremsenaryo),
    ncol(mahbinasayi),
    ncol(istanbuldepremverisi),
    ncol(belediyenufus),
    ncol(kentseldonusum)
  ),
  missing_values = c(
    sum(is.na(depremsenaryo)),
    sum(is.na(mahbinasayi)),
    sum(is.na(istanbuldepremverisi)),
    sum(is.na(belediyenufus)),
    sum(is.na(kentseldonusum))
  ),
  duplicate_rows = c(
    sum(duplicated(depremsenaryo)),
    sum(duplicated(mahbinasayi)),
    sum(duplicated(istanbuldepremverisi)),
    sum(duplicated(belediyenufus)),
    sum(duplicated(kentseldonusum))
  )
)

  data_quality,
  caption = "Data quality checks after preprocessing"
)

Show Code
# Aggregate the neighbourhood-level sources to one row per district.
depremsenaryo_ilce <- depremsenaryo |>
  group_by(ilce_adi) |>
  summarise(
    across(
      c(
        cok_agir_hasarli_bina_sayisi,
        agir_hasarli_bina_sayisi,
        orta_hasarli_bina_sayisi,
        hafif_hasarli_bina_sayisi,
        can_kaybi_sayisi,
        agir_yarali_sayisi,
        hastanede_tedavi_sayisi,
        hafif_yarali_sayisi,
        dogalgaz_boru_hasari,
        icme_suyu_boru_hasari,
        atik_su_boru_hasari,
        gecici_barinma
      ),
      ~ sum(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    mahalle_sayisi = n_distinct(mahalle_koy_uavt),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

mahbinasayi_ilce <- mahbinasayi |>
  group_by(ilce_adi) |>
  summarise(
    across(
      c(
        `1980_oncesi`,
        `1980-2000_arasi`,
        `2000_sonrasi`,
        `1-4 kat_arasi`,
        `5-9 kat_arasi`,
        `9-19 kat_arasi`
      ),
      ~ sum(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

belediyenufus_ilce <- belediyenufus |>
  transmute(
    ilce_adi,
    nufus_2019 = `2019`,
    nufus_2020 = `2020`,
    nufus_2021 = `2021`,
    nufus_2022 = `2022`,
    nufus_2023 = `2023`,
    nufus_2024 = `2024`
  )

kentseldonusum_ilce <- kentseldonusum |>
  transmute(
    ilce_adi,
    kentsel_donusum_evet = Kentsel_Donusum_Ihtiyac_Evet,
    kentsel_donusum_hayir = Kentsel_Donusum_Ihtiyac_Hayir
  )

# The observed earthquake file covers the wider region and has no district key.
# These measures are therefore retained as regional context, not district values.
istanbul_merkez_enlem <- 41.0082
istanbul_merkez_boylam <- 28.9784

istanbuldepremverisi <- istanbuldepremverisi |>
  mutate(
    istanbul_merkeze_uzaklik_km = 6371 * 2 * asin(
      sqrt(
        sin((Latitude - istanbul_merkez_enlem) * pi / 360)^2 +
          cos(istanbul_merkez_enlem * pi / 180) *
            cos(Latitude * pi / 180) *
            sin((Longitude - istanbul_merkez_boylam) * pi / 360)^2
      )
    )
  )

gozlenen_deprem_baglam <- istanbuldepremverisi |>
  summarise(
    bolgesel_deprem_sayisi = n(),
    istanbula_100km_deprem_sayisi =
      sum(istanbul_merkeze_uzaklik_km <= 100, na.rm = TRUE),
    ortalama_magnitud = mean(Magnitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    maksimum_magnitud = max(Magnitude, na.rm = TRUE),
    medyan_derinlik_km = median(`Depth/km`, na.rm = TRUE),
    ilk_deprem_tarihi = min(time, na.rm = TRUE),
    son_deprem_tarihi = max(time, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

# Join the four district-level sources and append the regional context.
ilce_birlesik <- depremsenaryo_ilce |>
  left_join(mahbinasayi_ilce, by = "ilce_adi") |>
  left_join(belediyenufus_ilce, by = "ilce_adi") |>
  left_join(kentseldonusum_ilce, by = "ilce_adi") |>
  cross_join(gozlenen_deprem_baglam) |>
  mutate(
    toplam_bina =
      `1980_oncesi` + `1980-2000_arasi` + `2000_sonrasi`,
    eski_bina_orani = if_else(
      toplam_bina > 0,
      `1980_oncesi` / toplam_bina,
      NA_real_
    ),
    kentsel_donusum_evet_orani = if_else(
      kentsel_donusum_evet + kentsel_donusum_hayir > 0,
      kentsel_donusum_evet /
        (kentsel_donusum_evet + kentsel_donusum_hayir),
      NA_real_
    ),
    nufus_degisim_orani_2019_2024 =
      (nufus_2024 - nufus_2019) / nufus_2019,
    can_kaybi_100bin =
      can_kaybi_sayisi / nufus_2024 * 100000,
    gecici_barinma_100bin =
      gecici_barinma / nufus_2024 * 100000,
    agir_ve_cok_agir_hasar_orani = if_else(
      toplam_bina > 0,
      (agir_hasarli_bina_sayisi +
        cok_agir_hasarli_bina_sayisi) / toplam_bina,
      NA_real_
    )
  )

# Every Istanbul district must appear exactly once after the joins.
stopifnot(
  nrow(ilce_birlesik) == 39,
  n_distinct(ilce_birlesik$ilce_adi) == 39,
  sum(is.na(ilce_birlesik)) == 0
)

# Save the cleaned source tables and the district-level analytical table.
save(
  depremsenaryo,
  mahbinasayi,
  istanbuldepremverisi,
  belediyenufus,
  kentseldonusum,
  depremsenaryo_ilce,
  mahbinasayi_ilce,
  belediyenufus_ilce,
  kentseldonusum_ilce,
  gozlenen_deprem_baglam,
  ilce_birlesik,
  file = "istanbul_deprem_verileri.RData"
)

title: Project Istanbul
text:
2.2 General Information About Data
Deprem Senaryosu Analiz Sonuçları is a dataset that contains the results of an earthquake scenario analysis. Its columns mainly include information on the number of buildings by damage level, the number of people by vital status, and pipe damage.
İstanbul Çevresinde Gerçekleşen Depremler 2019-2020 is a dataset on earthquakes that occurred around Istanbul. Each row represents a single earthquake, and the columns provide various details about these events.
2019-2024 Yılları Belediye Nüfusları is a dataset that provides population information for each municipality.
2017 Mahalle Bazlı Bina Sayıları is a dataset about buildings at the neighbourhood level. Each row represents a neighbourhood, and the columns mainly include information about the construction year of buildings and the number of floors.

title: Project Istanbul
text:
2.3 Reason of Choice
Istanbul is widely regarded as a city facing a very high earthquake risk, with the potential for severe loss of life and property. Experts frequently warn in the media that a major earthquake, estimated to range between magnitudes 7.2 and 7.6, could strike the city. I chose to focus on this highly relevant issue in order to inform the public through a comprehensive analysis, raise awareness, and encourage precautionary measures that could help minimize the impact of such a disaster.

title: Project Istanbul
text:
2.4 Preprocessing
Five datasets were downloaded from the sources listed in Section 2.1 and imported from their original Excel files using readxl. All character columns were converted to UTF-8, repeated whitespace was removed, and empty strings were represented as missing values. District names were standardized in Turkish uppercase and the word “Belediyesi” was removed from the population dataset to create a common ilce_adi key. The historical district name “Eyüp” was also standardized as “Eyüpsultan” to match the more recent datasets.
The earthquake timestamps were converted to UTC date-time values, while magnitude, depth, latitude, and longitude were explicitly checked as numeric variables. Fully duplicated rows were removed, and the district lists were validated across the earthquake scenario, building stock, population, and urban transformation datasets. The cleaned datasets were then stored in istanbul_deprem_verileri.RData.

title: Project Istanbul
text:
3.1 Exploratory Data Analysis
The exploratory analysis first examines the structure and quality of the integrated district-level dataset. Raw counts are considered together with population- and building-adjusted rates because districts differ considerably in population and building stock. The observed-earthquake variables are excluded from district comparisons because they describe the same regional context for every district.

3.1.1 Variable Dictionary
The following table documents the principal variables used in the district-level analysis. Count variables from the scenario and building datasets were aggregated by summing neighbourhood values within each district.

3.1.2 Data Quality Profile
The integrated table was checked for missing values, duplicated district records, impossible negative values, and variation across districts. Variables with only one unique value are regional context variables copied from the observed-earthquake dataset; they are retained for documentation but are not used in correlations or district rankings.


Show Code
eda_variables <- c(
  "can_kaybi_sayisi",
  "cok_agir_hasarli_bina_sayisi",
  "agir_hasarli_bina_sayisi",
  "gecici_barinma",
  "1980_oncesi",
  "toplam_bina",
  "eski_bina_orani",
  "nufus_2024",
  "nufus_degisim_orani_2019_2024",
  "kentsel_donusum_evet_orani",
  "can_kaybi_100bin",
  "gecici_barinma_100bin",
  "agir_ve_cok_agir_hasar_orani"
)

quality_profile <- ilce_birlesik |>
  select(all_of(eda_variables)) |>
  summarise(
    across(
      everything(),
      list(
        missing = ~ sum(is.na(.x)),
        unique = ~ n_distinct(.x),
        negative = ~ sum(.x < 0, na.rm = TRUE)
      ),
      .names = "{.col}__{.fn}"
    )
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = c("variable", ".value"),
    names_sep = "__"
  )

The substantial difference between the mean and median for projected loss of life indicates a right-skewed distribution: a small number of districts account for particularly high projected consequences. Building age also varies greatly. The median district has approximately 18.8% pre-1980 buildings, whereas the maximum district-level share exceeds 73%.


3.1.4 Distributions


Show Code
distribution_data <- ilce_birlesik |>
  select(
    `Projected loss of life` = can_kaybi_sayisi,
    `Loss of life per 100,000` = can_kaybi_100bin,
    `Pre-1980 building share` = eski_bina_orani,
    `2024 population` = nufus_2024,
    `Urban transformation need` = kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
    `Heavy damage share` = agir_ve_cok_agir_hasar_orani
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = "indicator",
    values_to = "value"
  )

ggplot(distribution_data, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ indicator, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Selected District Indicators",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of districts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









The histograms show that projected losses and damage are concentrated in the upper tail rather than distributed evenly across Istanbul. Population-adjusted loss therefore provides an important complement to raw loss counts, particularly for smaller but structurally vulnerable districts.


3.1.5 Outlier Assessment
Potential outliers were identified using the conventional 1.5 interquartile-range rule. These observations are not removed because they represent real districts and may be substantively important for disaster planning.


Show Code
find_iqr_outliers <- function(data, variable) {
  values <- data[[variable]]
  q1 <- quantile(values, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  q3 <- quantile(values, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  iqr_value <- q3 - q1

  data |>
    mutate(outlier_value = .data[[variable]]) |>
    filter(
      outlier_value < q1 - 1.5 * iqr_value |
        outlier_value > q3 + 1.5 * iqr_value
    ) |>
    transmute(
      variable = variable,
      ilce_adi,
      value = outlier_value
    )
}

outlier_variables <- c(
  "can_kaybi_sayisi",
  "cok_agir_hasarli_bina_sayisi",
  "agir_hasarli_bina_sayisi",
  "gecici_barinma",
  "1980_oncesi",
  "nufus_2024",
  "can_kaybi_100bin",
  "agir_ve_cok_agir_hasar_orani"
)

outlier_table <- bind_rows(
  lapply(
    outlier_variables,
    \(variable) find_iqr_outliers(ilce_birlesik, variable)
  )
) |>
  arrange(variable, desc(value))

  outlier_table,
  digits = 2,
  caption = "Potential district outliers based on the 1.5 IQR rule"
)

Spearman correlation is used because several indicators are strongly skewed and the sample consists of only 39 districts. The strongest relationship is between projected heavy-damage share and loss of life per 100,000 residents (\(\rho = 0.95\)). The pre-1980 building share has a moderate positive relationship with perceived urban transformation need (\(\rho = 0.55\)), while population change and transformation need have a strong negative relationship (\(\rho = -0.76\)). These correlations describe association, not causation. The scatter plot adds population and projected loss per 100,000 residents, allowing structural vulnerability, exposure, and consequences to be considered together.

title: Project Istanbul
text:
3.5 Results
The equal-weight composite score ranks Adalar, Fatih, Bakırköy, Küçükçekmece, and Bahçelievler as the five highest-risk districts. These districts reach high scores for different reasons. Adalar has the highest per-capita human-consequence and infrastructure scores despite having the lowest exposure score, while Fatih combines high human consequences with substantial structural vulnerability. Küçükçekmece and Bahçelievler have lower structural scores than Fatih but much larger exposed populations and building stocks.
Adalar’s first-place ranking requires particular care in interpretation. Its small population increases per-capita rates, so its score reflects high modeled consequences relative to population rather than the largest absolute number of affected people. For this reason, the component scores and raw counts should always be considered alongside the overall ranking.
The alternative weighting scheme produces a Spearman rank correlation of 0.965 with the equal-weight ranking, and nine of the ten highest-ranked districts remain in the top ten. This indicates that the broad ranking is relatively stable, although some individual districts change position when greater weight is assigned to human and structural consequences.

My first assignment has four parts.


A brief summary of Mr. Vardar’s speech
In my view, Mr. Vardar’s presentation can be divided into three primary parts.
First, he stresses that Industrial Engineers possess the skills required to effectively leverage data science tools and emphasizes the strong connection between Industrial Engineering and data science practices. He presents data analytics as a valuable problem-solving tool for Industrial Engineers.
Second, Mr. Vardar points out that in designing complex systems, simplicity is often the most effective approach. Even when complex solutions are necessary, they should ideally begin with simple foundations.
Lastly, he refers to the Pareto Principle (the 80-20 rule), explaining that mastering just 20% of the skills in data analytics such as programming, data mining techniques, or using cloud platforms can satisfy roughly 80% of an Industrial Engineer’s practical needs.



Question: How can Industrial Engineers leverage data science tools to solve real-world problems, and why is understanding both fields important for career development?
Answer: Industrial Engineers can use data science tools to analyze complex datasets and optimize processes. Understanding both Industrial Engineering and data science allows engineers to bridge technical problem-solving skills with data-driven insights.
Multiple-Choice Question: According to Cem Vardar, which statement about the Pareto Principle (80-20 rule) in learning data analytics is correct?

Industrial Engineers need to learn 100% of data analytics skills to be effective.

Learning 20% of key data analytics skills can satisfy 80% of the practical needs.

Only programming skills matter; other tools are not necessary.

The Pareto Principle does not apply to Industrial Engineers.

Answer: B

title: Assignment 1
text:
In my view, Mr. Vardar’s presentation can be divided into three primary parts.
First, he stresses that Industrial Engineers possess the skills required to effectively leverage data science tools and emphasizes the strong connection between Industrial Engineering and data science practices. He presents data analytics as a valuable problem-solving tool for Industrial Engineers.
Second, Mr. Vardar points out that in designing complex systems, simplicity is often the most effective approach. Even when complex solutions are necessary, they should ideally begin with simple foundations.
Lastly, he refers to the Pareto Principle (the 80-20 rule), explaining that mastering just 20% of the skills in data analytics such as programming, data mining techniques, or using cloud platforms can satisfy roughly 80% of an Industrial Engineer’s practical needs.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Question: How can Industrial Engineers leverage data science tools to solve real-world problems, and why is understanding both fields important for career development?
Answer: Industrial Engineers can use data science tools to analyze complex datasets and optimize processes. Understanding both Industrial Engineering and data science allows engineers to bridge technical problem-solving skills with data-driven insights.
Multiple-Choice Question: According to Cem Vardar, which statement about the Pareto Principle (80-20 rule) in learning data analytics is correct?

Industrial Engineers need to learn 100% of data analytics skills to be effective.

Learning 20% of key data analytics skills can satisfy 80% of the practical needs.

Only programming skills matter; other tools are not necessary.

The Pareto Principle does not apply to Industrial Engineers.

Answer: B

title:
EMU Hacettepe
EMU660 - Analitik Yöntemlerle Karar Verme
2025 – 2026 Bahar Dönemi
Ara Sınav
text:
EMU Hacettepe’nin bölüm başkanı Prof. Dr. Murat Caner Testik, mezun verilerinden yararlanarak bölümün kariyer hizmetlerini geliştirmek istemektedir. Caner hoca, analitik yöntemlerle karar verme alanındaki yetkinliğinize güvendiği için yardımınıza başvurmaktadır. Bu doğrultuda, EMU Hacettepe’nin myEMU Öğrenci ve Mezun Takip Sisteminden sağlanan myemu_data isimli veri setini sizinle paylaşmaktadır.

myemu_data, bölüm lisans programından mezun olan ve iş deneyimlerine sahip olan mezunlara ait bilgileri içeren bir veri setidir.
Veri seti, mezunların, mezuniyet not ortalamalarına (CGPA), geçmiş iş tecrübelerine ve öğrenciyken katıldıkları Erasmus programına dair detayları barındırır. Erasmus katılım sütunundaki 1 değeri, ilgili kişinin Erasmus programına katıldığını belirtir.
Bir mezunun, birden fazla iş tecrübesi olabileceği için veri setinde birden fazla kaydı (satırı) olabilir. Aynı kişiye ait her bir satırda farklı iş tecrübesi olsa da, not ortalaması ve erasmus gibi bilgiler aynı kişiye ait her satırda tekrar etmektedir.

Caner hoca, özellikle mezunların iş deneyimi örüntüleri ve sektör bazlı kariyer yollarıyla ilgilenmektedir. Bu bilgiler, bölümün kariyer danışmanlığı hizmetlerini iyileştirmek için kullanılacaktır. Artık verileri analiz etmeye hazırsınız!

text:
EMU Hacettepe mezun verisini R’a yükleyin
Caner hoca veri setini size .RData dosyası (myemu_data.RData) olarak vermiştir. Veri dosyasına www.hadi.hacettepe.edu.tr’de, ders sayfamızda, sınav gönderiminden erişebilirsiniz. Dosyayı, R quarto dosyanızla aynı klasöre koyunuz.

text:
Görev 1. Temel Veri Keşfi
Isınmak için basit görevlerle başlıyorsunuz.
Veri setinde kaç tane benzersiz (unique) şirket (work_company) var? (sonucu yazdırın)

unique_companies <- length(unique(myemu_data$work_company))
print(unique_companies)

Veri setinde kaç tane benzersiz sektör (work_industry) var? (sonucu yazdırın)

unique_industries <- length(unique(myemu_data$work_industry))
print(unique_industries)

[1] 76


Her bir mezunun kaç farklı iş deneyimi kaydı olduğunu hesaplayınız. Sonuçları jobs_per_graduate isimli bir veri çerçevesinde (data.frame) saklayınız. Bu veri çerçevesinde student_id ve job_count (iş sayısı) sütunları bulunmalıdır.
İpucu 1: group_by() ile student_id’ye göre gruplayın.
İpucu 2: summarise() ile n() fonksiyonunu kullanarak her öğrencinin iş sayısını hesaplayın.

 jobs_per_graduate <- myemu_data |>
   group_by(student_id) |>
   summarise(job_count =n())

section: Görev 3. Mezuniyet Yılına Göre CGPA Analizi
text:
Görev 3. Mezuniyet Yılına Göre CGPA Analizi
Caner Hoca, yıllar içinde mezunların akademik performansının nasıl değiştiğini merak etmektedir. Mezuniyet yılına göre CGPA dağılımını inceleyeceksiniz.

graduation_year sütunu ekleyin.
Veri setinde graduation_date sütunu GG/AA/YYYY formatındadır. Ancak size sadece yıl bilgisi gereklidir.

İlk olarak, lubridate kütüphanesinden dmy() fonksiyonunu kullanarak graduation_date’i tarihe dönüştürün.
Sonra, year() fonksiyonu ile yılı çıkarın.
Son olarak graduation_year adında yeni bir sütun olarak veri çerçevenize ekleyin.
Sonraki analizlerde tutarlılık için graduation_year’ı faktör tipi bir değişken (sütun) olarak saklayınız.


 myemu_data <- myemu_data |>
   mutate(graduation_year = as.factor(year(dmy(graduation_date))))



Yıl Bazında Özet İstatistikleri Hesapla
Her mezuniyet yılı için mezun sayısı, medyan CGPA, minimum CGPA ve maksimum CGPA hesaplayın.
İpucu 1: Her mezunun sadece ilk görünümünü korumak için distinct() fonksiyonunu kullanın (student_id sütununa göre, keep_all = TRUE ile).
İpucu 2: group_by() ile graduation_year’a göre gruplayın.
İpucu 3: summarise() ile gerekli istatistikleri hesaplayın.
İpucu 4: Mezun sayısını bulmak için n() fonksiyonunu kullanın.

 myemu_data |>
   distinct(student_id, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
   group_by(graduation_year) |>
   summarise(
     count_graduates = n(),
     median_cgpa = median(graduation_cgpa, na.rm= TRUE),
     min_cgpa = min(graduation_cgpa, na.rm= TRUE),
     max_cgpa = max(graduation_cgpa, na.rm= TRUE)
   )

Yıl Bazında Mezun Sayısı Çubuk Grafiği Oluştur
Her yılda kaç mezun olduğunu gösteren bir çubuk grafiği oluşturun.
İpucu 1: Her mezunun yalnızca bir kez temsil edilmesi için distinct() kullanın.
İpucu 2: geom_bar() kullanın (sayımı ggplot otomatik yapacaktır).
İpucu 3: Yılları farklı renklerle göstermek için fill argümanını kullanın.

 myemu_data |>
   distinct(student_id, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
   ggplot(aes(x = graduation_year, fill = graduation_year)) +
   geom_bar() +
   labs(title = "Number of Graduates by Year",
        x = "Graduation Year",
        y = "Number of Graduates") +
   theme_minimal() +
   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))










Her Mezuniyet Yılı İçin Yoğunluk Grafiği (Density Plot) Çizme
CGPA dağılımını yıllar bazında görebilmek için yoğunluk grafiği oluşturun. facet_wrap() ile her yıl için ayrı bir panel oluşturun.
İpucu 1: Her mezunun yalnızca bir kez temsil edilmesi için distinct() kullanın.
İpucu 2: geom_density() kullanın ve fill = "blue", alpha = 0.5 argümanlarını ekleyin.
İpucu 3: x ekseninde graduation_cgpa yer alacaktır.
İpucu 4: facet_wrap() ile graduation_year’a göre yüzeyleme yapın.

 myemu_data |>
   distinct(student_id, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
   ggplot(aes(x = graduation_cgpa)) +
   geom_density(fill = "blue", alpha = 0.5) +
   facet_wrap(~ graduation_year) +
   labs(title = "Density of Graduation CGPA by Year",
        x = "Graduation CGPA",
        y = "Density") +
   theme_minimal()








Yoğunluk grafiklerine dayanarak hangi yılda mezun olanların CGPA dağılımı en geniş yayılıma sahiptir? Yıllar arasında belirgin bir CGPA farkı gözlemliyor musunuz?






Cevap:




2017 daha geniş bir yayılıma sahip görünüyor. Genel olarak 2.5-3.0 bandında yoğunlaşıyor, belirgin bir fark yok.

section: Görev 5: Erasmus Katılımı ve Sektör Tercih İlişkisi
text:
Görev 5: Erasmus Katılımı ve Sektör Tercih İlişkisi
Caner Hoca, Erasmus programına katılan ve katılmayan mezunların sektör tercihlerinde farklılık olup olmadığını merak etmektedir.

Erasmus Katılım Faktörü Oluştur
Veri setinde erasmus_participation sütunu, Erasmus programına katılım durumunu göstermektedir. Katılımcılar için bu sütun 1 değerini alırken, programa katılmayanlar için sütunda NA değeri bulunmaktadır. ifelse() ve is.na() fonksiyonlarını iç içe kullanarak, NA değerlerini "Hayır", NA olmayanları (1’leri) "Evet" olarak dönüştürün ve sonuçları erasmus_factor isimli yeni bir sütunda faktör türünde kaydedin.

 myemu_data <- myemu_data |>
   mutate(erasmus_factor = factor(ifelse(is.na(erasmus_participation), "Hayır", "Evet")))



En Çok İstihdam Kaydına Sahip 6 Sektör İçin Erasmus Katılım Oranı Hesapla
Önce en çok istihdam kaydına sahip 6 sektörü belirleyin. Sonra bu sektörler için Erasmus’a katılan mezunların oranını hesaplayın.
İpucu 1: En çok kayda sahip 6 sektörü belirlemek için önceki görevdeki yaklaşımı kullanın.
İpucu 2: Bu sektörlerdeki verileri filter() ile filtreleyin.
İpucu 3: group_by(work_industry) ile gruplayın.
İpucu 4: summarise() içinde mean(erasmus_factor == "Evet") ile oranı hesaplayın.

 top6_industries <- myemu_data |>
   count(work_industry) |>
   slice_max(n, n = 6)

 erasmus_by_industry <- myemu_data |>
   filter(work_industry %in% top6_industries$work_industry) |>
   group_by(work_industry) |>
   summarise(erasmus_rate = mean(erasmus_factor == "Evet")) |>
   arrange(desc(erasmus_rate))

title: Project Istanbul
text:
2.5 District-Level Data Integration
The earthquake scenario and building stock datasets originally contain one row per neighbourhood. Their count variables were summed to produce one row for each district. The population and urban transformation datasets already contain one row per district, so only their variable names and district keys were standardized.
The observed earthquake dataset cannot be assigned directly to Istanbul districts because its coordinates cover a much wider region, including Western Türkiye, the Aegean Sea, the Black Sea, Bulgaria, and surrounding areas. Assigning these events to Istanbul districts would therefore be misleading. Instead, this fifth dataset contributes regional context variables, including the total number of observed earthquakes, the number recorded within 100 km of central Istanbul, mean and maximum magnitude, median depth, and the observation period. These contextual values are identical for all district rows and must not be interpreted as district-specific earthquake counts.
The resulting ilce_birlesik table contains exactly one row for each of Istanbul’s 39 districts. It combines projected earthquake consequences, building characteristics, annual population, urban transformation opinions, and regional observed-earthquake context. It also includes derived variables for the proportion of pre-1980 buildings, the proportion reporting a need for urban transformation, and population change between 2019 and 2024.


Show Code
integration_summary <- tibble(

    ncol(ilce_birlesik),
    sum(is.na(ilce_birlesik)),
    sum(duplicated(ilce_birlesik$ilce_adi))
  )
)

title: Project Istanbul
text:
3.2 Normalized District Risk Score

3.2.1 Method
A composite score was developed to compare the relative earthquake risk of Istanbul’s districts. The score does not estimate the probability that an earthquake will occur. Instead, it combines the projected consequences, structural vulnerability, infrastructure and shelter pressure, and the scale of exposed population and buildings under the scenario data.
Each indicator was normalized to the 0–100 range using min–max normalization:
\[
z_{ij} = 100 \times
\frac{x_{ij} - \min(x_j)}
{\max(x_j) - \min(x_j)}
\]
where \(x_{ij}\) is the value of district \(i\) for indicator \(j\). A value of 0 represents the lowest observed value among the 39 districts and 100 represents the highest. Indicators within each dimension were averaged, and the four dimension scores were assigned equal weights. Equal weights were chosen because no expert-derived weighting scheme was available.


Show Code
risk_method <- tribble(
  ~dimension, ~weight, ~indicators,
  "Human consequences", "25%",
  "Loss of life, severe injuries, and hospital treatment per 100,000 residents",
  "Structural vulnerability", "25%",
  "Heavy/severe damage share and pre-1980 building share",
  "Infrastructure and shelter", "25%",
  "Temporary shelter and natural gas, drinking-water, and wastewater damage per 100,000 residents",
  "Exposure", "25%",
  "2024 population and total building stock"
)

knitr::kable(
  risk_method,
  caption = "Dimensions and weights of the normalized district risk score"
)

3.2.2 Calculation


Show Code
min_max_100 <- function(x) {
  limits <- range(x, na.rm = TRUE)

  if (limits[1] == limits[2]) {
    return(rep(0, length(x)))
  }

  100 * (x - limits[1]) / (limits[2] - limits[1])
}

ilce_risk <- ilce_birlesik |>
  mutate(
    agir_yarali_100bin =
      agir_yarali_sayisi / nufus_2024 * 100000,
    hastane_tedavi_100bin =
      hastanede_tedavi_sayisi / nufus_2024 * 100000,
    dogalgaz_hasari_100bin =
      dogalgaz_boru_hasari / nufus_2024 * 100000,
    icme_suyu_hasari_100bin =
      icme_suyu_boru_hasari / nufus_2024 * 100000,
    atik_su_hasari_100bin =
      atik_su_boru_hasari / nufus_2024 * 100000,
    norm_can_kaybi = min_max_100(can_kaybi_100bin),
    norm_agir_yarali = min_max_100(agir_yarali_100bin),
    norm_hastane_tedavi = min_max_100(hastane_tedavi_100bin),
    norm_agir_hasar = min_max_100(agir_ve_cok_agir_hasar_orani),
    norm_eski_bina = min_max_100(eski_bina_orani),
    norm_gecici_barinma = min_max_100(gecici_barinma_100bin),
    norm_dogalgaz = min_max_100(dogalgaz_hasari_100bin),
    norm_icme_suyu = min_max_100(icme_suyu_hasari_100bin),
    norm_atik_su = min_max_100(atik_su_hasari_100bin),
    norm_nufus = min_max_100(nufus_2024),
    norm_toplam_bina = min_max_100(toplam_bina),
    insani_sonuc_puani = rowMeans(
      pick(
        norm_can_kaybi,
        norm_agir_yarali,
        norm_hastane_tedavi
      )
    ),
    yapisal_kirilganlik_puani = rowMeans(
      pick(norm_agir_hasar, norm_eski_bina)
    ),
    altyapi_barinma_puani = rowMeans(
      pick(
        norm_gecici_barinma,
        norm_dogalgaz,
        norm_icme_suyu,
        norm_atik_su
      )
    ),
    maruziyet_puani = rowMeans(
      pick(norm_nufus, norm_toplam_bina)
    ),
    risk_puani = rowMeans(
      pick(
        insani_sonuc_puani,
        yapisal_kirilganlik_puani,
        altyapi_barinma_puani,
        maruziyet_puani
      )
    ),
    risk_sirasi = min_rank(desc(risk_puani)),
    risk_grubu_no = ntile(desc(risk_puani), 5),
    risk_grubu = factor(
      recode(
        risk_grubu_no,
        `1` = "Very high",
        `2` = "High",
        `3` = "Moderate",
        `4` = "Low",
        `5` = "Very low"
      ),
      levels = c("Very high", "High", "Moderate", "Low", "Very low")
    )
  ) |>
  arrange(risk_sirasi)

stopifnot(
  nrow(ilce_risk) == 39,
  all(between(ilce_risk$risk_puani, 0, 100)),
  n_distinct(ilce_risk$risk_sirasi) == 39
)

    altyapi_barinma_puani,

  ) |>
  slice_head(n = 15) |>
  mutate(
    across(
      ends_with("_puani"),
      ~ round(.x, 1)
    )
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "The 15 districts with the highest normalized risk scores"
  )

Risk groups are relative quintiles rather than absolute safety thresholds. A district classified as “very low” therefore has lower modeled risk than the other Istanbul districts, but it should not be interpreted as having no earthquake risk.


3.2.3 Ranking and Risk Components


Show Code
top_risk_districts <- ilce_risk |>
  slice_head(n = 15)

ggplot(
  top_risk_districts,
  aes(
    x = risk_puani,
    y = fct_reorder(ilce_adi, risk_puani),
    fill = risk_grubu
  )
) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = round(risk_puani, 1)),
    hjust = -0.15,
    size = 3.2
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Very high" = "#9b2226",
      "High" = "#ee9b00",
      "Moderate" = "#e9d8a6",
      "Low" = "#94d2bd",
      "Very low" = "#0a9396"
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, max(top_risk_districts$risk_puani) * 1.12)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Districts with the Highest Composite Risk Scores",
    x = "Normalized risk score (0-100)",
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Relative risk group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Show Code
risk_components <- ilce_risk |>
  slice_head(n = 15) |>
  select(
    ilce_adi,
    `Human consequences` = insani_sonuc_puani,
    `Structural vulnerability` = yapisal_kirilganlik_puani,
    `Infrastructure and shelter` = altyapi_barinma_puani,
    Exposure = maruziyet_puani
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    -ilce_adi,
    names_to = "dimension",
    values_to = "score"
  )

ggplot(
  risk_components,
  aes(
    x = dimension,
    y = fct_rev(factor(
      ilce_adi,
      levels = top_risk_districts$ilce_adi
    )),
    fill = score

) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(score)), size = 3) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(
    title = "Risk Components of the 15 Highest-Ranked Districts",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))











3.2.4 Weight Sensitivity
An alternative score was calculated by assigning 35% to human consequences, 30% to structural vulnerability, 20% to infrastructure and shelter, and 15% to exposure. This alternative reflects a stronger priority on preventing loss of life and structural damage.


Show Code
ilce_risk <- ilce_risk |>
  mutate(
    alternatif_risk_puani =
      0.35 * insani_sonuc_puani +
      0.30 * yapisal_kirilganlik_puani +
      0.20 * altyapi_barinma_puani +
      0.15 * maruziyet_puani,
    alternatif_sira = min_rank(desc(alternatif_risk_puani)),
    sira_farki = alternatif_sira - risk_sirasi
  )

rank_correlation <- cor(
  ilce_risk$risk_puani,
  ilce_risk$alternatif_risk_puani,
  method = "spearman"
)

sensitivity_summary <- tibble(
  measure = c(
    "Spearman rank correlation",
    "Districts remaining in the top 10",
    "Largest absolute rank change"
  ),
  value = c(
    round(rank_correlation, 3),
    length(intersect(
      ilce_risk$ilce_adi[ilce_risk$risk_sirasi <= 10],
      ilce_risk$ilce_adi[ilce_risk$alternatif_sira <= 10]
    )),
    max(abs(ilce_risk$sira_farki))
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  sensitivity_summary,
  caption = "Sensitivity of the district ranking to alternative weights"
)

The sensitivity analysis indicates whether the ranking is stable when greater importance is assigned to human and structural consequences. Nevertheless, the score remains a decision-support index based on the available scenario data, not an official earthquake-risk classification. Expert consultation would be required before using the weights for operational resource allocation.

title: Project Istanbul
text:
3.3 Urban Transformation Opinions and Risk
The urban transformation variable represents the proportion of respondents in each district who reported that their household needed urban transformation. It is compared below with model-based risk indicators derived from the earthquake scenario, building stock, infrastructure, and population datasets. These indicators are objective in the sense that they are calculated from administrative and scenario data, but they should not be interpreted as observed post-earthquake damage.

3.3.1 Association with Risk Indicators


Show Code
transformation_correlations <- ilce_risk |>
  summarise(
    `Overall risk score` = cor(
      kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
      risk_puani,
      method = "spearman"
    ),
    `Human consequences` = cor(
      kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
      insani_sonuc_puani,
      method = "spearman"
    ),
    `Structural vulnerability` = cor(
      kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
      yapisal_kirilganlik_puani,
      method = "spearman"
    ),
    `Infrastructure and shelter` = cor(
      kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
      altyapi_barinma_puani,
      method = "spearman"
    ),
    Exposure = cor(
      kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
      maruziyet_puani,
      method = "spearman"
    ),
    `Pre-1980 building share` = cor(
      kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
      eski_bina_orani,
      method = "spearman"
    ),
    `Heavy damage share` = cor(
      kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
      agir_ve_cok_agir_hasar_orani,
      method = "spearman"
    )
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = "risk_indicator",
    values_to = "spearman_rho"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(spearman_rho)) |>
  mutate(spearman_rho = round(spearman_rho, 3))

risk_transformation_test <- cor.test(
  ilce_risk$risk_puani,
  ilce_risk$kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
  method = "spearman",
  exact = FALSE
)

  transformation_correlations,
  caption = "Relationship between urban transformation opinions and risk indicators"
)

Perceived urban transformation need has a positive but modest relationship with the overall risk score (\(\rho = 0.339\), \(p = 0.035\)). The relationship is stronger for the pre-1980 building share (\(\rho = 0.554\)) and structural vulnerability component (\(\rho = 0.524\)). In contrast, perceived need is almost unrelated to exposure and infrastructure pressure. Residents’ opinions therefore appear to align more closely with visible building age than with the complete multidimensional risk profile.


3.3.2 Risk-Demand Policy Groups
Districts were divided using the median overall risk score and median urban transformation-need proportion. This creates four relative policy groups. The “high risk / low demand” group is particularly important because the modeled risk is above the Istanbul median while perceived transformation need is below the median.


Show Code
median_risk <- median(ilce_risk$risk_puani)
median_transformation <- median(
  ilce_risk$kentsel_donusum_evet_orani
)

ilce_risk <- ilce_risk |>
  mutate(
    politika_grubu = case_when(
      risk_puani >= median_risk &
        kentsel_donusum_evet_orani >= median_transformation ~
        "High risk / high demand",
      risk_puani >= median_risk &
        kentsel_donusum_evet_orani < median_transformation ~
        "High risk / low demand",
      risk_puani < median_risk &
        kentsel_donusum_evet_orani >= median_transformation ~
        "Low risk / high demand",
      TRUE ~ "Low risk / low demand"
    ),
    politika_grubu = factor(
      politika_grubu,
      levels = c(
        "High risk / low demand",
        "High risk / high demand",
        "Low risk / high demand",
        "Low risk / low demand"
      )
    ),
    risk_yuzdelik = percent_rank(risk_puani),
    donusum_talebi_yuzdelik =
      percent_rank(kentsel_donusum_evet_orani),
    risk_talep_farki =
      risk_yuzdelik - donusum_talebi_yuzdelik
  )

policy_group_summary <- ilce_risk |>
  count(politika_grubu, name = "districts")

knitr::kable(
  policy_group_summary,
  caption = "Distribution of districts across risk-demand policy groups"
)

label_districts <- ilce_risk |>
  filter(
    politika_grubu == "High risk / low demand" |
      risk_sirasi <= 5

ggplot(
  ilce_risk,
  aes(
    x = kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
    y = risk_puani,
    color = politika_grubu,
    size = nufus_2024
  )
) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = median_transformation,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey50"
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = median_risk,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey50"
  ) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(
    data = label_districts,
    aes(label = ilce_adi),
    size = 2.8,
    vjust = -0.8,
    show.legend = FALSE,
    check_overlap = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent) +
  scale_size_continuous(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale())
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "High risk / low demand" = "#9b2226",
      "High risk / high demand" = "#ee9b00",
      "Low risk / high demand" = "#0a9396",
      "Low risk / low demand" = "#94d2bd"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Modeled Risk and Perceived Urban Transformation Need",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines show the median values across 39 districts",
    x = "Share reporting a need for urban transformation",
    y = "Normalized risk score",
    color = "Policy group",
    size = "2024 population"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Nine districts fall into the high-risk/low-demand group. This does not prove that residents are unaware of risk: the opinion data may reflect financial constraints, tenure status, trust, previous transformation experience, or differences in how the survey question was understood. It does, however, identify districts where technical assessment and public perception are less closely aligned.


3.3.3 Priority Gaps
The risk-demand gap compares each district’s percentile position in the risk ranking with its percentile position in transformation demand. Positive values indicate that a district ranks higher in modeled risk than in perceived transformation need. This is a relative prioritization measure, not an estimate of individual awareness.


Show Code
high_risk_low_demand <- ilce_risk |>
  filter(politika_grubu == "High risk / low demand") |>
  arrange(desc(risk_puani)) |>
  transmute(
    district = ilce_adi,
    risk_rank = risk_sirasi,
    risk_score = round(risk_puani, 1),
    transformation_need =
      percent(kentsel_donusum_evet_orani, accuracy = 0.1),
    structural_score =
      round(yapisal_kirilganlik_puani, 1),
    pre_1980_share =
      percent(eski_bina_orani, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

3.4.3 District Boundary Integration
The district map uses GADM 4.1 second-level administrative boundaries. A fixed lookup based on GADM geographic identifiers is used because several district names in the source file have character-encoding differences. The code stops if the boundary data and risk table do not match exactly.


Show Code
suppressWarnings(
  suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(sf))
)

turkey_district_boundaries <- st_read(
  "data/gadm/gadm41_TUR_2.json",
  quiet = TRUE
)

istanbul_boundary_lookup <- c(
  "TUR.40.1_1" = "ADALAR",
  "TUR.40.2_1" = "ARNAVUTKÖY",
  "TUR.40.3_1" = "ATAŞEHİR",
  "TUR.40.4_1" = "AVCILAR",
  "TUR.40.5_1" = "BAĞCILAR",
  "TUR.40.6_1" = "BAHÇELİEVLER",
  "TUR.40.7_1" = "BAKIRKÖY",
  "TUR.40.8_1" = "BAŞAKŞEHİR",
  "TUR.40.9_1" = "BAYRAMPAŞA",
  "TUR.40.10_1" = "BEŞİKTAŞ",
  "TUR.40.11_1" = "BEYKOZ",
  "TUR.40.12_1" = "BEYLİKDÜZÜ",
  "TUR.40.13_1" = "BEYOĞLU",
  "TUR.40.14_1" = "BÜYÜKÇEKMECE",
  "TUR.40.15_1" = "ÇATALCA",
  "TUR.40.16_1" = "ÇEKMEKÖY",
  "TUR.40.17_1" = "ESENLER",
  "TUR.40.18_1" = "ESENYURT",
  "TUR.40.19_1" = "EYÜPSULTAN",
  "TUR.40.20_1" = "FATİH",
  "TUR.40.21_1" = "GAZİOSMANPAŞA",
  "TUR.40.22_1" = "GÜNGÖREN",
  "TUR.40.23_1" = "KADIKÖY",
  "TUR.40.24_1" = "KAĞITHANE",
  "TUR.40.25_1" = "KARTAL",
  "TUR.40.26_1" = "KÜÇÜKÇEKMECE",
  "TUR.40.27_1" = "MALTEPE",
  "TUR.40.28_1" = "PENDİK",
  "TUR.40.29_1" = "SANCAKTEPE",
  "TUR.40.30_1" = "SARIYER",
  "TUR.40.31_1" = "ŞİLE",
  "TUR.40.32_1" = "ŞİŞLİ",
  "TUR.40.33_1" = "SULTANBEYLİ",
  "TUR.40.34_1" = "SULTANGAZİ",
  "TUR.40.35_1" = "TUZLA",

  "TUR.40.39_1" = "SİLİVRİ"
)

istanbul_ilce_haritasi <- turkey_district_boundaries |>
  filter(GID_2 %in% names(istanbul_boundary_lookup)) |>
  mutate(
    ilce_adi = unname(istanbul_boundary_lookup[GID_2])
  ) |>
  select(ilce_adi, geometry) |>
  left_join(ilce_risk, by = "ilce_adi")

stopifnot(
  nrow(istanbul_ilce_haritasi) == 39,
  n_distinct(istanbul_ilce_haritasi$ilce_adi) == 39,
  setequal(
    istanbul_ilce_haritasi$ilce_adi,
    ilce_risk$ilce_adi
  ),
  sum(is.na(istanbul_ilce_haritasi$risk_puani)) == 0,
  all(st_is_valid(istanbul_ilce_haritasi))
)




3.4.4 Static Risk Map


Show Code
ggplot(istanbul_ilce_haritasi) +
  geom_sf(
    aes(fill = risk_puani),
    color = "white",
    linewidth = 0.25
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "magma",
    direction = -1,
    name = "Risk score"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Normalized Earthquake Risk Score by District",
    subtitle = "Relative composite score based on four equally weighted dimensions",
    caption = "District boundaries: GADM 4.1"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right"
  )









The map highlights clusters that are less visible in a simple ranking. High scores appear in both central districts with older building stocks and western districts with high projected consequences or exposure. The map is comparative: lighter districts have lower scores relative to other Istanbul districts, not zero earthquake risk.


3.4.5 Interactive District Map


Show Code
suppressWarnings(library(leaflet))

risk_palette <- colorNumeric(
  palette = "YlOrRd",
  domain = istanbul_ilce_haritasi$risk_puani,
  reverse = FALSE
)

district_popup <- sprintf(
  paste0(
    "<strong>%s</strong><br>",
    "Risk rank: %s / 39<br>",
    "Risk score: %.1f<br>",
    "Risk group: %s<br>",
    "Human consequences: %.1f<br>",
    "Structural vulnerability: %.1f<br>",
    "Infrastructure and shelter: %.1f<br>",
    "Exposure: %.1f<br>",
    "Projected loss of life: %s<br>",
    "2024 population: %s<br>",
    "Transformation need: %s<br>",
    "Risk-demand group: %s"
  ),
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$ilce_adi,
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$risk_sirasi,
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$risk_puani,
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$risk_grubu,
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$insani_sonuc_puani,
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$yapisal_kirilganlik_puani,
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$altyapi_barinma_puani,
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$maruziyet_puani,
  comma(istanbul_ilce_haritasi$can_kaybi_sayisi),
  comma(istanbul_ilce_haritasi$nufus_2024),
  percent(
    istanbul_ilce_haritasi$kentsel_donusum_evet_orani,
    accuracy = 0.1
  ),
  istanbul_ilce_haritasi$politika_grubu
)

leaflet(istanbul_ilce_haritasi) |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~ risk_palette(risk_puani),
    fillOpacity = 0.75,
    color = "white",
    weight = 1,
    popup = district_popup,
    label = ~ paste0(
      ilce_adi,
      ": ",
      round(risk_puani, 1)
    ),
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(
      weight = 3,
      color = "#333333",
      fillOpacity = 0.9,
      bringToFront = TRUE
    )
  ) |>
  addLegend(
    pal = risk_palette,
    values = ~ risk_puani,
    title = "Risk score",
    position = "bottomright",
    opacity = 0.8
  ) |>
  fitBounds(
    lng1 = 28.05,
    lat1 = 40.80,
    lng2 = 29.90,
    lat2 = 41.56
  )
